sentences.text: Of course there was an overt showing of Nazism.
There were, they were marching, brown shirts, marching with flags, and sometimes at night with torch-- torch light parades.
There was much of that.
You know, all kinds of assemblies and all kinds of sieg heil, and so on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I felt shut out.
I, you know, I-- I didn't know enough to actually oppose it.
And there was so much enthusiasm by people who were taken in by it that it was pretty difficult.
One way I felt shut out is when I was still in, in primary school, there were people who were allowed to carry the flags, and, and, and, and you know, greet other people.
And you always had to heil Hitler.
And in, in a class, a parallel class, in the same grade, the children voted for who should carry the flag.
And the most popular girl won the vote.
Except that she was Jewish so was not allowed to carry the flag.
We would never allowed to carry-- I mean, in retrospect, my parents would have been shocked if I carried a, a swastika.
But it was that kind of thing, where you know, they would-- well, they, they would-- they sent children with a message to another teacher, into another class.
And you had to come in and say, heil Hitler, I'm sent by the teacher.
And I was sent once and-- and did this.
And my parents were absolutely shocked.
But that was what we would told to do.
And because everything, every letter started with, with heil Hitler.
And, and, and everything was geared to that.
The whole populace had been totally indoctrinated.
Sorry, bring me back to you know, how did I feel, what did I see.
I-- this is-- it was just the ordinary [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] street scene.
You got used to that.
One thing is Mainz is a predominantly Catholic town.
And the carnival, the days before Lent, are a very, very big deal.
I mean, the-- the school is out, and there is, there's a big parade, with-- with all kinds of costumes.
It's almost like the Mummer's parade, you know.
And everybody participates.
And in the olden days, we certainly watched.
And, and, and there were-- there were balls, formal balls.
And my parents used to go to those.
They'd get dressed up to the nines and went.
And, of course, that stopped.
But, the-- I mean, they stopped going there.
But the parades went on.
And my grandparents' house was right on the parade route, so that we had very desirable windows.
And we could no longer watch with the curtains drawn.
We had to watch from behind the drawn curtains because they knew that Jewish people lived there.
But-- I mean this was, this was one kind of parade, which was a very benign kind.
This is a folk festival in Mainz.
It-- it always was.
It still is.
And-- but in those days, it-- it too, had been politicized.
This was from-- during the Nazi time, from '33 till '39.
I left in '39.
And I take it they even had them during the war, these parades.
And, and they've resumed it, in a big way.

It's, it's, it's part of the-- you know, I mean it is amazing how Germany has bounced back after those 12 years-- which Hitler wanted to be 1,000-- to its old ways and, and as if nothing had ever happened.
Except the Jews aren't there anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Jews had been active in those parades and those balls and, and all that kind of thing.
That is one of my most vivid memories.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As I said, we-- school was right next to the synagogue.
And I remember that the maid came in.
I guess we still had a maid in those days, or maybe it was an older person.
She came in and said the synagogue is burning.
And this was November 10.
And I, you know, got up, and got dressed, and said, well, you know, I don't believe it, whatever it was.
Though we had heard that, that, you know, this assassination had occurred in Paris.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And--
That's right, yeah.
And, but of course, the whole thing had been organized by the Nazis, to have this reaction throughout all of Germany.
I mean, this was quite obvious.
But we didn't know that.
We, you know, we thought somebody had just started to burn our synagogue.
And my father said, well I'll come with you, just to make sure you're, you're all right.
And, and he, and I, and my friend-- we had the same-- she lived next door and we went to school together.
We went just to this-- to where the school had been.
And of course, the synagogue was burning.
And the fire-- firemen stood by and watched it and mainly protected the houses around it.
And my father told us to go home immediately.
And he ran to notify the rabbi, who, of course, knew about this.
I mean, my-- we were so naive that my father must have thought, well, I better tell the rabbi.
But the rabbi know about it.
But he ran.
And I remember people jeering because my father was running.
And I was-- it, it worried me.
I thought somebody was going to do something to him.

Well my father actually-- when he got through with the rabbi-- he went to Frankfurt, which was the nearest big town-- it still is the nearest big town-- but the town where there was an American consulate.
And at that point got the visa number for my grandparents.
Because he realized this was the end.
This was the abs-- the beginning of the absolute end.
In Frankfurt, they were arresting people on the streets.
Fortunately, they didn't know my father.
So my father came home, you know, all in one piece, for the time being.
So we were just sitting at home.
It was my grandfather's 77th birthday.
And we were going to have a little family supper.
And I remember distinctly distracting myself, by decorating a potato salad.
And of course, we were all sort of sitting together and-- and, you know, trembling.

And I remember that the, the crowds of school children who were being let out of school for the occasion, walking around, throwing things, invading homes, throwing things out of-- looting, throwing furniture out of the windows, you know, cracking the windows.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Just unspeakable, you know, invasions of people's private homes and stores, also.
Jewish stores, stores owned by Jews.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the, the miracle was that my grandparents' home and my parents' home were not loot-- were not hurt.
But I still have a visceral reaction to mobs, mob noise and, and, and crashing crystal.
I mean, it's just never left me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I later on went to my grandparents' house, I saw part of the mob coming on the other side-- or on the same side.
And I crossed over and quickly walked the other way.
But well, we-- we-- I told you about how we listened to the radio that night and, and-- to see what the foreign radio would say.
And they were reporting it, but they were-- and-- and then we realized it was taking place all over Germany, of course.
After my father came home from Frankfurt, he had seen it in, in Frankfurt.
And we suddenly realized that it was totally organized and was taking place all over the place.
And the next day was a Friday.
And on Friday nights, we always went to my grandparents' for dinner and shabbat.
And-- now I have to say few words about my grandmother.
My grandmother had three sons.
And two of them were killed in the first World War.
So my father was the only survivor.
She never got over this, of course.
She and my father were very, very close.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to get back to what happened the next day, the, the knock on-- the famous knock on the door.
And the Gestapo was there and said to my father to come along.
So my father threw a few things in a suitcase and came-- and, and wrote on the back of an English language teaching book, you know, notify the cousins in America immediately.
Whatever they can do.
This is no, no more time to say depression or what have you in America.
You know, we've got to have a way of getting out of this country immediately.
And, and they took him along.
So this was just before suppertime on Friday night.
And I said to my mother, what are we going to tell the grandparents?
We were practically on our way to the grandparents to have supper with them.
And my-- I've told you about my grandmother.
My grandfather had had a heart ailment for years.
And my mother says, nothing we can do but tell them the truth.
We, we have to tell them that, you know.
So we went there.
And we told my grandparents.
And my grandmother [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] put on her hat.
[SOBBING] This is a difficult part.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And she said, we go to the Gestapo.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my grandmother and my grandfather-- she said to my grandfather, [SPEAKING GERMAN], which [GERMAN] means treasure.
She always called my grandfather that.
So my grand-- my grandparents and my mother and I and the little brown dachshund went to the Gestapo, which was not very far away, the building of the Gestapo.
And my grandmother said, let me handle this.
She went in and you know, it seemed ages until she came back out.
But she did go back out.
And she said, it's all right.
They're going to release him.
And we said, what did, what did you say?
And she said, I told them [SOBBING] I gave two sons for my country.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I want my-- I demand.
She went to the Gestapo and, I demand my third one back.
And they said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And we had gone just a few paces.
[SOBBING] And he actually followed us home and immediately took to his bed.
And he said, you know, anybody comes, I'm sick.
But she actually saved him.
Because he wouldn't have survived in the concentration camp.
Frank's father went to concentration camp and never talked about it.
My father was a very gentle person and very righteous and would just not have made it.
And so, when-- because-- and then we suddenly had an invasion of people whose homes had been destroyed-- my aunt and uncle, cousin, relative strangers.
I mean, we took people in, just they were all over the house.
Because they had no place to go, and, and we had a, a, a functioning household.
So I remember several people.
I mean, aside from, from, from relatives who came to, to, to be with us and stay with us as long as was-- as long as they had to.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so that was basically my experience of Kristallnacht.
I mean, I know that the, the Jews had to go and clean up their own-- the mess that other people had made of their homes and of their stores and then were fined in addition to that.
That was the law, the Nazi law.
And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] that, you know, that, that was another reason my, my father was so close to his mother.
I mean he was-- you know, it just-- more and more, this closeness built up.
And he was never able to get her out of, of Germany.
My grandfather died in, in the summer of 1940 of natural causes.
He was 78, I guess.
Simon Gaertner, S-I-M-O-N.
And, and then she was all alone.
She was 81 at the time.
Or 82.
Julina, J-U-L-I-N-A.
Also called Julschen, which is sort of a diminutive in German.
And she was from a town called Nierstein nearby, and, and also been in the wine business for a long time.
But anyway, my father tried, literally everything, from the time we came here, to get her out of, out of Germany.
He tried to get her transferred to our quota number.
Because my mother was still there, and it was going through.
And I would like to tell you about my mother's experiences, too.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He wrote to the President of the United States, to, to try and get this quota number.
Of course, I mean, it was all for nothing.
It was-- in a way, he knew it.
He wasn't that naive that he didn't, but he just could not leave any stone unturned trying to get her to come to this country, trying to save her.
He tried to get her to Cuba, which is what Frank's parents did.
And that no longer worked.
And of course once, once we were-- in-- after Pearl Harbor, once we were in the war, there was no way of doing it anymore.
I can't say that for sure.
They were just going around and-- they were not our neighbors.
They were a face-- basically to me, at least-- a faceless mob.
I mean, this is, you know, you're talking about a town the size of Trenton.
You don't, you don't know everybody who's going around.
I do not think they were the middle class people.
I mean, I don't-- I still don't think that ordinary, middle class people will go around in mobs, in looting.
They may have been children of middle class people who didn't know any better, who came with their classmates or what have you.
But they were, you know, rabble, mob.
They were, they were not, you know, not educated people, I would hope.
I don't know.
I think I was incredulous and terrified.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I, I don't, I don't, I can't really remember anything beside that, except, you know, what's next.
What do we do now?
And actually, what happened next was that everybody realized it was the beginning of the end.
In fact, the town of Mainz has issued a, a set of books entitled, And it is about the Jewish community of the time.
And, and everybody-- the only thing anybody talked about, from then on, is how to get away, how to get out of Germany.
You know, some people were luckier than others in that they had American visas with, with lower numbers.
Some people went to Shanghai.
Some people went to Israel.
Anything to get out.
And you know, many people just got stuck.
My, my aunt and uncle just got stuck.
They, they didn't have a high enough quota number.
They had been beaten black and blue in the town where they lived, actually beaten up in Hanau, in their town.
And they fled to our-- they were part of the people who came to live with us and never went back to Hanau that I know of.
Hanau is not very far away.
H-A-N-A-U.
It's a little industrial town, on the other side of Frankfurt, east of Mainz.
I think it had happened once before.
But it certainly happened at Kristallnacht, and they came.
And my cousin's husband came.
And my cousin came.
And, they were-- and I don't know where she was.
She may have been in England at the time.
But anyway, they, they all sort of settled.
And I had my, my other aunt and uncle also lived in Mainz.
So we were able to take care of, of, of the family, certainly.
But everybody just wanted to you know, get out of Germany.
And there were very few ways, in which you could get out legally.
I mean, if you crossed the-- if you took a chance and crossed the border, then you were taking a chance.
And some people, they never made it.
But one legal way of doing it was to have some time in England, which is the-- what my father did.
He applied to live in England for-- until the visa came through.
And in order to have something to live on, my mother's uncle, who lived in San Francisco, who had lived there since before the earthquake in 1906.
He had immigrated there.
Adolph Oppenheimer, A-D-O-L-P-H Oppenheimer, her father's brother.
He saved maybe 16 people.
I mean, he saved the bulk of the family.
He was a bachelor, and he made money available.
He gave affidavits of support and just-- until, until the American authorities said you can't possibly support all these people.
So he was not able to support my-- I mean to-- to give an affidavit for my aunt and uncle.
He might have been able to give it had their number not been so high.
But, but you know, their-- they, they just had too high a number.
But he was still able out of his savings to make money available for my father to live frugally in England for a year.
The reason my father went without my mother is that my-- it was more dangerous for men, obviously.
The men were being taken into concentration camps first.
And so the men had to get out.
And so he went out with the idea that my mother-- that he could possibly interest someone in England to take my mother on as a domestic servant.
And the-- and I was in England-- I'll tell you about my going to England-- at the time.
But he came over to England two weeks before the war started in England.
So of course there was no way of getting my mother into, into England.
So my mother sweated it out in Germany, until her number came due.
Well, I think the expectation at that time already was of immigration.
I mean, it was, you know, it was-- but we had a wonderful time in that school where we were.
And we put on English plays.
And we put on other plays.
And we had dances, and we had sports, athletic events.
And so I don't think as a 14-year-old I had that much perception of what the future was.
I think things are different now.
I, I think people are-- young people are encouraged to think about the future, think about college and so on and so forth.
I was a relatively easy-going child.
And I don't think I ever had any expectations except to go to school until I was, you know, had made all the requisite exams.
And then I would do something.
For one thing, a woman wasn't expected to, to go into any kind of a career.
And so I had never thought of a career, which you know, was different in the case of men, of young men, who-- who-- who started planning for the future.
Well, by the time it actually-- when push came to shove, it was a great adventure.
I think [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my parents were very fortunate in finding friends of friends who knew where-- how I could get to Ger-- to England.
There was a-- they had found a school where a-- well, in England, there had been an appeal.
I found this out afterwards.
But-- in fact, fairly recently-- when after Kristallnacht, I mean, when the English-- some of the English people in charge realized what was going on, they wanted to save the young people.
They decided at least if we can't save the grownups, let's save some of the young people.
And they organized these famous kinder transports.
Kinder transporter.
OK.
That's fine with me because I have to go to the bathroom.
